credit_headline.narrative: Bank credit hit an all-time high of ₹213.6L Cr in March 2026, growing 16.1% YoY — the fastest pace on record and a clear re-acceleration from 14.6% in January. FY26 added ₹29.6L Cr, the largest annual increment ever, with ₹29.2L Cr coming from non-food lending. All four main sectors are growing simultaneously, confirming a synchronized credit cycle. This is not a one-quarter blip: FY26 growth accelerated 5.1pp over FY25 and has held that pace for six straight periods. Banks are deploying capital at peak velocity across productive sectors — NBFCs and fintechs face intensified competition for quality borrowers, and regulators should watch for asset quality stress as volumes compound.

sibc-bank-credit-abs.title: Bank credit at ₹213.6L Cr — highest on record
sibc-bank-credit-abs.observation: Total bank credit outstanding reached ₹213.6L Cr in March 2026, the highest level in this dataset.
sibc-bank-credit-abs.direction: Volume rose ₹6.1L Cr from February 2026 (₹207.5L Cr → ₹213.6L Cr), continuing a steady climb from ₹178.7L Cr in January 2025.
sibc-bank-credit-abs.inference: Credit deployment is at peak levels with sustained sequential growth. Banks are expanding balance sheets aggressively — watch for asset quality and capital adequacy headroom as volumes compound.

sibc-bank-credit-fy-acceleration.title: FY26 acceleration holds at 5.1pp — strongest in window
sibc-bank-credit-fy-acceleration.observation: Full-year credit growth accelerated by 5.1 percentage points from FY25 to FY26, unchanged from prior period.
sibc-bank-credit-fy-acceleration.direction: Acceleration has held steady at 5.1pp for six consecutive periods since January 2025, the maximum in this series.
sibc-bank-credit-fy-acceleration.inference: The credit cycle shifted into a higher gear in FY26 and has sustained that momentum. This is not a one-quarter spike — it reflects a structural re-rating of credit demand or supply appetite.

sibc-bank-credit-fy-delta.title: FY26 added ₹29.6L Cr — largest annual increment
sibc-bank-credit-fy-delta.observation: Banks deployed ₹29.6L Cr of incremental credit in FY26, the highest absolute addition in this dataset.
sibc-bank-credit-fy-delta.direction: The ₹29.6L Cr FY26 increment has remained constant across all six reporting periods, confirming the full-year tally.
sibc-bank-credit-fy-delta.inference: This is the biggest single-year credit expansion on record. Origination engines are running at full capacity — NBFCs and fintechs should expect intensified competition for quality borrowers.

sibc-bank-credit-yoy.title: Bank credit YoY at 16.1% — re-accelerating from 14.6%
sibc-bank-credit-yoy.observation: Year-on-year credit growth reached 16.1% in March 2026, the fastest rate in this four-period window.
sibc-bank-credit-yoy.direction: Growth re-accelerated from 14.6% in January 2026 to 16.1% in March 2026, reversing the prior deceleration from 11.4% (January 2025) to 11.0% (March 2025).
sibc-bank-credit-yoy.inference: Credit growth has broken out of its prior slowdown and is now expanding at the highest pace on record. This signals strong loan demand or aggressive bank origination — either way, credit availability is peaking.

sibc-food-credit-abs.title: Food credit at ₹0.70L Cr — down ₹0.12L Cr from February
sibc-food-credit-abs.observation: Food credit outstanding stood at ₹0.70L Cr in March 2026, near the median of the five-period range.
sibc-food-credit-abs.direction: Volume declined ₹0.12L Cr from February 2026 (₹0.83L Cr → ₹0.70L Cr), following a sharp drop from ₹0.89L Cr in January 2026.
sibc-food-credit-abs.inference: Food credit shows high volatility with no clear trend. The sequential decline suggests seasonal procurement cycle effects or policy-driven drawdowns — not a structural credit signal.

sibc-food-credit-yoy.title: Food credit YoY surged to 139.4% — up 80pp from January
sibc-food-credit-yoy.observation: Food credit grew 139.4% year-on-year in March 2026, the highest rate in this four-period series.
sibc-food-credit-yoy.direction: Growth exploded from 58.9% in January 2026 to 139.4% in March 2026, a jump of 80.4 percentage points.
sibc-food-credit-yoy.inference: This extreme spike reflects a low base in March 2025 (₹0.29L Cr) rather than sustained credit expansion. Food credit is policy-driven and volatile — do not extrapolate this growth rate.

sibc-nonfood-credit-abs.title: Non-food credit at ₹212.9L Cr — new peak
sibc-nonfood-credit-abs.observation: Non-food credit outstanding reached ₹212.9L Cr in March 2026, the highest level in this five-period dataset.
sibc-nonfood-credit-abs.direction: Volume rose ₹6.2L Cr from February 2026 (₹206.7L Cr → ₹212.9L Cr), extending a steady climb from ₹178.1L Cr in January 2025.
sibc-nonfood-credit-abs.inference: Non-food credit — the core productive lending pool — is at record highs and growing steadily. This is where the real credit action is: industry, services, retail, and agriculture ex-food.

sibc-nonfood-credit-fy-delta.title: Non-food FY26 add at ₹29.2L Cr — maximum on record
sibc-nonfood-credit-fy-delta.observation: Banks added ₹29.2L Cr of non-food credit in FY26, the largest annual increment in this six-period series.
sibc-nonfood-credit-fy-delta.direction: The ₹29.2L Cr FY26 addition has held constant across all six reporting periods, confirming the full-year tally.
sibc-nonfood-credit-fy-delta.inference: Nearly all of the record FY26 credit expansion (₹29.6L Cr total) came from non-food lending. Banks are prioritising productive sectors over food procurement — a sign of broad-based economic credit demand.

sibc-nonfood-credit-yoy.title: Non-food credit YoY at 15.9% — re-accelerating from 14.4%
sibc-nonfood-credit-yoy.observation: Non-food credit grew 15.9% year-on-year in March 2026, the fastest rate in this four-period window.
sibc-nonfood-credit-yoy.direction: Growth re-accelerated from 14.4% in January 2026 to 15.9% in March 2026, reversing the prior deceleration from 11.3% (January 2025) to 10.9% (March 2025).
sibc-nonfood-credit-yoy.inference: Non-food credit is driving the overall credit surge. With growth above 15%, banks are aggressively deploying capital into productive sectors — NBFCs must compete on speed and underwriting to capture share.

sibc-sectors-positive-yoy-count.title: All 4 main sectors growing YoY — breadth at maximum
sibc-sectors-positive-yoy-count.observation: All four main credit sectors (agriculture, industry, services, retail) posted positive year-on-year growth in March 2026.
sibc-sectors-positive-yoy-count.direction: Breadth has remained at 4 out of 4 sectors for six consecutive periods since January 2025, the maximum possible.
sibc-sectors-positive-yoy-count.inference: Credit expansion is broad-based, not concentrated. Every major sector is growing — this is a synchronized credit cycle, reducing single-sector concentration risk but also signaling peak deployment across the board.

psl.narrative: Priority sector lending is bifurcating sharply. Housing (39.8% YoY) and Renewable Energy (34.1%) are pulling away as the dominant growth engines, with Micro & Small Enterprises close behind at 29.5%. Export Credit has now contracted for two straight periods at -8.4%, and Weaker Sections credit — a core inclusion mandate — is growing at only 11.1%, the weakest among positive categories. The 49.4pp spread between top and bottom reflects a structural tilt: banks are chasing housing and green finance mandates aggressively, while traditional inclusion segments and trade finance lag. For lenders, housing and renewables offer the clearest PSL compliance path with strong demand tailwinds; for policymakers, the weak Weaker Sections and export credit numbers flag gaps in financial inclusion and trade support.

sibc-psl-agri-yoy.title: PSL Agriculture at 16.0% YoY — highest in this window
sibc-psl-agri-yoy.observation: PSL Agriculture credit grew 16.0% YoY in Mar 2026, the strongest reading across all six periods.
sibc-psl-agri-yoy.direction: Growth accelerated from 11.0% in Mar 2025 to 16.0% in Mar 2026, with steady strengthening from 14.1% → 15.2% → 16.0% over the past three periods.
sibc-psl-agri-yoy.inference: Agriculture lending is in a sustained uptrend, gaining momentum each period. This is the strongest growth rate on record in this window — banks are expanding agri exposure at an accelerating pace.

sibc-psl-education-yoy.title: PSL Education loans surged to 23.0% YoY from near-zero
sibc-psl-education-yoy.observation: PSL Educational Loans grew 23.0% YoY in Mar 2026, up from 0.9% in Mar 2025.
sibc-psl-education-yoy.direction: Growth jumped from 0.9% → 23.5% → 24.0% → 23.0% across the latest three periods, a sharp inflection from the prior year's near-stagnant 2.2% → 1.2% → 0.9% trajectory.
sibc-psl-education-yoy.inference: Education lending has reignited after a year of dormancy. The 20+ percentage point acceleration signals renewed bank appetite for this segment, likely driven by policy push or demand recovery.

sibc-psl-export-yoy.title: PSL Export credit -8.4% YoY — decline moderating sharply
sibc-psl-export-yoy.observation: PSL Export Credit contracted 8.4% YoY in Mar 2026, the least severe decline in the current downturn.
sibc-psl-export-yoy.direction: Contraction eased from -17.2% → -14.1% → -8.4% over the past three periods, after growing 5.3% in Mar 2025.
sibc-psl-export-yoy.inference: Export credit is still shrinking, but the pace of decline is halving each period. If this trajectory holds, the segment could return to positive growth within one to two periods.

sibc-psl-housing-yoy.title: PSL Housing at 39.8% YoY — record high growth
sibc-psl-housing-yoy.observation: PSL Housing credit grew 39.8% YoY in Mar 2026, the highest rate in this six-period window.
sibc-psl-housing-yoy.direction: Growth surged from -1.1% in Mar 2025 to 37.9% → 38.2% → 39.8% across the latest three periods, a complete reversal from prior-year contraction.
sibc-psl-housing-yoy.inference: Housing is the standout PSL segment — banks are deploying capital aggressively here. The 40-point swing from negative to near-40% growth marks a structural shift in priority sector allocation.

sibc-psl-medium-yoy.title: PSL Medium Enterprises at 17.8% YoY — lowest in window
sibc-psl-medium-yoy.observation: PSL Medium Enterprises credit grew 17.8% YoY in Mar 2026, the weakest reading across all six periods.
sibc-psl-medium-yoy.direction: Growth decelerated from 22.6% in Mar 2025 to 18.9% → 18.4% → 17.8% over the past three periods.
sibc-psl-medium-yoy.inference: Medium enterprises are losing momentum while Micro & Small surge. Banks may be shifting PSL capital toward smaller borrowers or other higher-growth segments like Housing.

sibc-psl-msme-fy-acceleration.title: PSL MSME acceleration held at 16.1pp — maximum level
sibc-psl-msme-fy-acceleration.observation: PSL MSME credit accelerated by 16.1 percentage points on a full-year basis, unchanged from the prior period.
sibc-psl-msme-fy-acceleration.direction: The acceleration metric has remained flat at 16.1pp across all six periods, indicating the full-year growth rate comparison is stable at its peak.
sibc-psl-msme-fy-acceleration.inference: MSME credit growth is sustaining its highest full-year acceleration on record. This is not a one-period spike — the structural uplift is entrenched.

sibc-psl-msme-yoy.title: PSL Micro & Small at 29.5% YoY — record high
sibc-psl-msme-yoy.observation: PSL Micro & Small enterprises credit grew 29.5% YoY in Mar 2026, the strongest rate in this window.
sibc-psl-msme-yoy.direction: Growth more than doubled from 13.4% in Mar 2025 to 24.8% → 26.5% → 29.5% over the past three periods.
sibc-psl-msme-yoy.inference: Micro & Small is the fastest-growing PSL segment, accelerating every period. The 16-point jump from Mar 2025 confirms a structural inflection — banks are prioritizing smaller borrowers at scale.

sibc-psl-others-yoy.title: PSL Others -9.6% YoY — contraction easing rapidly
sibc-psl-others-yoy.observation: PSL Others contracted 9.6% YoY in Mar 2026, the least severe decline in this window.
sibc-psl-others-yoy.direction: Contraction narrowed sharply from -19.2% in Mar 2025 to -23.9% → -19.4% → -9.6% over the past three periods.
sibc-psl-others-yoy.inference: The 'Others' bucket is still shrinking, but the decline is halving. This could signal runoff of legacy exposures or a shift in classification — watch for a return to positive growth next period.

sibc-psl-renewable-pos-streak.title: PSL Renewable Energy grew for 6 straight periods — longest run
sibc-psl-renewable-pos-streak.observation: PSL Renewable Energy has posted positive YoY growth for six consecutive periods, the longest streak on record in this window.
sibc-psl-renewable-pos-streak.direction: The streak extended from 1 → 2 → 3 → 4 → 5 → 6 periods without interruption.
sibc-psl-renewable-pos-streak.inference: Renewable Energy lending is in a sustained expansion phase. The unbroken streak signals consistent bank commitment to this segment, despite recent volatility in growth rates.

sibc-psl-renewable-yoy.title: PSL Renewable Energy at 34.1% YoY — sharp deceleration
sibc-psl-renewable-yoy.observation: PSL Renewable Energy grew 34.1% YoY in Mar 2026, the lowest rate in this six-period window.
sibc-psl-renewable-yoy.direction: Growth decelerated sharply from 78.3% in Mar 2025 to 62.0% → 64.4% → 34.1% over the past three periods.
sibc-psl-renewable-yoy.inference: Renewable Energy is still growing strongly, but the pace has halved from its peak. The deceleration may reflect base effects from prior-year surges or a normalization after an initial policy-driven spike.

sibc-psl-social-infra-yoy.title: PSL Social Infrastructure at 12.9% YoY — rebounded sharply
sibc-psl-social-infra-yoy.observation: PSL Social Infrastructure grew 12.9% YoY in Mar 2026, up from -1.0% in the prior period.
sibc-psl-social-infra-yoy.direction: Growth swung from -1.0% in Feb 2026 to 12.9% in Mar 2026, a 14-point reversal in one period.
sibc-psl-social-infra-yoy.inference: Social Infrastructure lending is highly volatile — the series shows wild swings from 61.9% to -1.0% to 12.9%. The latest rebound suggests episodic large disbursements rather than steady growth.

sibc-psl-weaker-sections-yoy.title: PSL Weaker Sections at 11.1% YoY — lowest in window
sibc-psl-weaker-sections-yoy.observation: PSL Weaker Sections credit grew 11.1% YoY in Mar 2026, the weakest reading across all six periods.
sibc-psl-weaker-sections-yoy.direction: Growth decelerated from 15.3% in Mar 2025 to 11.9% → 13.5% → 11.1% over the past three periods.
sibc-psl-weaker-sections-yoy.inference: Weaker Sections lending is losing momentum while other PSL segments surge. Banks may be meeting regulatory targets but not expanding beyond minimums — watch for compliance risk if this trend continues.

sibc-psl-yoy-scan.title: Housing 39.8%, Renewable Energy 34.1% lead PSL — Export Credit -8.4%
sibc-psl-yoy-scan.observation: Housing grew 39.8% YoY, Renewable Energy 34.1%, and Micro & Small Enterprises 29.5% — the three fastest-growing PSL categories.
sibc-psl-yoy-scan.direction: Top three categories all strengthening or active. Export Credit contracted -8.4%, Others -9.6%. Spread between fastest and slowest: 49.4 percentage points.
sibc-psl-yoy-scan.inference: PSL growth is concentrating in housing and green finance, with micro-enterprise credit also surging. Export credit remains in structural decline for a second consecutive period. Weaker Sections at 11.1% — the slowest among positive categories — signals uneven priority sector momentum despite headline strength in housing and renewables.

industry.narrative: Industry credit is undergoing a historic rebalancing. Micro & Small credit surged 33.1% YoY — the highest in this window — driven by a 24.2pp full-year acceleration that has held at maximum for six straight periods. This segment now holds 23.2% of industry credit, up 3.2pp year-on-year, while large corporates have lost 3.8pp of share despite re-accelerating to 8.9% growth. The MSME-Large spread has widened from 2.1pp to 24.2pp in one year, signaling a structural shift toward smaller borrowers. Within textiles, all four sub-segments are strengthening, led by Jute at 16.5% and Other Textiles at 15.9%, indicating broad-based sector recovery.

sibc-basic-metal-sub-share-scan.title: Iron & Steel 68.8% of Basic Metal — share rising
sibc-basic-metal-sub-share-scan.observation: Iron and Steel holds 68.8% of Basic Metal credit, Other Metal and Metal Products 31.2%.
sibc-basic-metal-sub-share-scan.direction: Iron and Steel shifted from active to strengthening; Other Metal and Metal Products moved from active to weakening.
sibc-basic-metal-sub-share-scan.inference: Iron and Steel is consolidating dominance within Basic Metal — the 37.7 point spread is widening as the larger segment gains share. Lenders are concentrating exposure in the core steel segment.

sibc-basic-metal-sub-yoy-scan.title: Other Metal 21.0% YoY — outpacing Iron & Steel at 18.7%
sibc-basic-metal-sub-yoy-scan.observation: Other Metal and Metal Products grew 21.0% YoY, Iron and Steel 18.7%.
sibc-basic-metal-sub-yoy-scan.direction: Both segments growing in the high teens; Other Metal leads by 2.2 points.
sibc-basic-metal-sub-yoy-scan.inference: Despite Iron and Steel's larger share, Other Metal is growing faster — a sign of diversification within Basic Metal credit. The narrow spread suggests balanced momentum across the sub-sector.

sibc-chemicals-sub-share-scan.title: Pharma 33.9% of Chemicals — share rising, Fertiliser 9.9% falling
sibc-chemicals-sub-share-scan.observation: Drugs and Pharmaceuticals holds 33.9% of Chemicals credit, Fertiliser 9.9%, Petro Chemicals 11.9%, Others 44.2%.
sibc-chemicals-sub-share-scan.direction: Drugs and Pharmaceuticals shifted from active to strengthening; Fertiliser moved from strengthening to weakening; Petro Chemicals recovered from weakening to active.
sibc-chemicals-sub-share-scan.inference: Pharma is gaining share within Chemicals as Fertiliser loses ground — a structural shift toward higher-margin, export-oriented segments. Petro Chemicals stabilising after prior weakness.

sibc-chemicals-sub-yoy-scan.title: Petro Chemicals 27.7% YoY — Fertiliser contracts -4.5%
sibc-chemicals-sub-yoy-scan.observation: Petro Chemicals grew 27.7% YoY, Drugs and Pharmaceuticals 17.9%, Others 14.9%, Fertiliser -4.5%.
sibc-chemicals-sub-yoy-scan.direction: Petro Chemicals leads growth; Fertiliser shifted from strengthening to declining — the only contracting segment.
sibc-chemicals-sub-yoy-scan.inference: Fertiliser credit is shrinking as subsidy-linked lending weakens, while Petro Chemicals surges — likely driven by capacity expansion and export demand. Pharma's 17.9% growth reinforces its share gain.

sibc-engineering-sub-share-scan.title: Others 81.1% of Engineering — Electronics 18.9% losing share
sibc-engineering-sub-share-scan.observation: Others holds 81.1% of All Engineering credit, Electronics 18.9%.
sibc-engineering-sub-share-scan.direction: Others shifted from active to strengthening; Electronics moved from active to weakening — the 62.2 point spread is widening.
sibc-engineering-sub-share-scan.inference: Non-electronics engineering segments are pulling away — likely capital goods, machinery, and industrial equipment. Electronics is losing share despite absolute growth.

sibc-engineering-sub-yoy-scan.title: Engineering Others 37.5% YoY — Electronics 13.3% lagging
sibc-engineering-sub-yoy-scan.direction: Others accelerating sharply; Electronics shifted from strengthening to active — growth moderating. Spread widened to 24.2 points.
sibc-engineering-sub-yoy-scan.inference: Capital goods and industrial machinery are driving Engineering credit — Electronics growth is slowing as the sector matures. The 37.5% surge in Others signals capex-driven lending.

sibc-food-processing-sub-share-scan.title: Others 75.7% of Food Processing — Edible Oils 10.4% gaining share
sibc-food-processing-sub-share-scan.observation: Others holds 75.7% of Food Processing credit, Sugar 11.9%, Edible Oils and Vanaspati 10.4%, Tea 2.0%.
sibc-food-processing-sub-share-scan.direction: Edible Oils shifted from active to strengthening; Sugar moved from strengthening to active; Others weakening despite largest share.
sibc-food-processing-sub-share-scan.inference: Edible Oils is gaining share as Others loses ground — a shift toward commodity-linked segments. Sugar's share stabilising after prior gains.

sibc-food-processing-sub-yoy-scan.title: Edible Oils 23.9% YoY — Tea 0.8% slowest in Food Processing
sibc-food-processing-sub-yoy-scan.observation: Edible Oils and Vanaspati grew 23.9% YoY, Others 14.8%, Sugar 4.3%, Tea 0.8%.
sibc-food-processing-sub-yoy-scan.direction: Edible Oils shifted from active to strengthening; Tea recovered from declining to strengthening but remains slowest. Spread 23.1 points.
sibc-food-processing-sub-yoy-scan.inference: Edible Oils is the growth driver in Food Processing — likely import substitution and export demand. Tea's 0.8% growth signals structural stagnation despite exiting contraction.

sibc-industry-type-share-scan.title: Infrastructure 32.6% of industry credit — Basic Metal 11.3%, Other 7.6%
sibc-industry-type-share-scan.observation: Infrastructure holds 32.6% of industry credit, Basic Metal and Metal Products 11.3%, Other Industries 7.6%.
sibc-industry-type-share-scan.direction: All 19 industry types remain active — no status shifts. Spread 32.3 points from top to bottom.
sibc-industry-type-share-scan.inference: Infrastructure dominates industry credit by a wide margin — one-third of all lending. The stable status across all segments suggests broad-based but uneven growth, with Infrastructure pulling away.

sibc-industry-type-yoy-scan.title: Gems & Jewellery 41.4% YoY — Mining 7.2% slowest among 19 types
sibc-industry-type-yoy-scan.observation: Gems and Jewellery grew 41.4% YoY, Petroleum and Coal Products 32.5%, All Engineering 32.2%, Mining and Quarrying 7.2%.
sibc-industry-type-yoy-scan.direction: 12 of 19 types strengthening, 7 active. All Engineering, Chemicals, Beverage and Tobacco shifted from strengthening to active; Glass and Glassware, Construction moved from active to strengthening.
sibc-industry-type-yoy-scan.inference: Gems and Jewellery leads all industry types — export-driven surge. Engineering, Chemicals, and Beverage growth moderating after prior acceleration. Mining's 7.2% signals structural weakness in extractives.

sibc-infra-sub-share-scan.title: Power 56.6% of Infrastructure — Roads 22.6%, Telecom 5.4%
sibc-infra-sub-share-scan.observation: Power holds 56.6% of Infrastructure credit, Roads 22.6%, Other Infrastructure 12.0%, Telecom 2.6%.
sibc-infra-sub-share-scan.direction: Power shifted from strengthening to active; Roads moved from weakening to active. All 7 sub-segments remain active.
sibc-infra-sub-share-scan.inference: Power dominates Infrastructure lending — more than half of all credit. Roads stabilising after prior share loss. The 56.1 point spread confirms Power's structural primacy.

sibc-infra-sub-yoy-scan.title: Ports 48.7% YoY — Railways -50.1%, Airports -27.2% contracting
sibc-infra-sub-yoy-scan.observation: Ports grew 48.7% YoY, Power 22.1%, Roads 1.1%, Telecom -12.2%, Airports -27.2%, Railways -50.1%.
sibc-infra-sub-yoy-scan.direction: Roads shifted from declining to strengthening; Ports moved from strengthening to active. 4 of 7 sub-segments declining.
sibc-infra-sub-yoy-scan.inference: Ports surging on export infrastructure build-out; Power sustaining strong growth. Railways and Airports in sharp contraction — likely project completion or repayment cycles. Roads barely positive after exiting decline.

sibc-large-corporate-share.title: Large corporate share at 67.1% — down 3.8pp from year-ago
sibc-large-corporate-share.observation: Large corporates account for 67.1% of industry credit, effectively flat from 67.1% last period.
sibc-large-corporate-share.direction: Share fell from 70.9% in Mar 2025 to 67.1% in Mar 2026 — a 3.8 percentage point decline year-on-year.
sibc-large-corporate-share.inference: Large corporates are losing share as MSME credit surges. The shift is structural: share has compressed from 71.0% to 67.1% over six periods, driven by faster growth in smaller segments.

sibc-large-corporate-yoy.title: Large corporate credit at 8.9% YoY — highest in this window
sibc-large-corporate-yoy.observation: Large corporate credit grew 8.9% year-on-year, the strongest reading in the six-period series.
sibc-large-corporate-yoy.direction: Growth accelerated from 7.8% last period to 8.9%, and from 6.9% in Mar 2025 to 8.9% in Mar 2026.
sibc-large-corporate-yoy.inference: Large corporate credit is re-accelerating despite losing share. The segment is growing in absolute terms, but MSME is growing three times faster — hence the share compression.

sibc-msme-medium-share.title: Medium enterprises hold 9.7% share — up 0.6pp year-on-year
sibc-msme-medium-share.observation: Medium enterprises account for 9.7% of industry credit, unchanged from 9.7% last period.
sibc-msme-medium-share.direction: Share rose from 9.1% in Mar 2025 to 9.7% in Mar 2026, a 0.6 percentage point gain.
sibc-msme-medium-share.inference: Medium enterprises are gaining share steadily, driven by 21.7% YoY growth that outpaces large corporates by 12.8 points. The segment is consolidating its position in the upper quartile of its historical range.

sibc-msme-medium-yoy.title: Medium enterprises at 21.7% YoY — sustained above 21%
sibc-msme-medium-yoy.observation: Medium enterprise credit grew 21.7% year-on-year, up from 21.0% last period.
sibc-msme-medium-yoy.direction: Growth accelerated from 18.5% in Mar 2025 to 21.7% in Mar 2026, and has held above 21% for two consecutive periods.
sibc-msme-medium-yoy.inference: Medium enterprises are in a sustained high-growth phase, expanding more than twice as fast as large corporates. This is a structural shift, not a spike — growth has been above 18% for six straight periods.

sibc-msme-micro-small-fy-acceleration.title: Micro & Small FY acceleration at 24.2pp — maximum on record
sibc-msme-micro-small-fy-acceleration.observation: Micro & Small credit accelerated by 24.2 percentage points on a full-year basis, unchanged from last period.
sibc-msme-micro-small-fy-acceleration.direction: The acceleration has held at 24.2pp for all six periods in this series — the highest level recorded.
sibc-msme-micro-small-fy-acceleration.inference: This is the single largest structural shift in the industry credit dataset. Micro & Small grew 8.9% in FY25 and 33.1% in FY26 — a 24.2pp jump that signals a policy-driven or demand-driven inflection. The acceleration is not moderating.

sibc-msme-micro-small-share.title: Micro & Small at 23.2% share — up 3.2pp year-on-year
sibc-msme-micro-small-share.observation: Micro & Small enterprises account for 23.2% of industry credit, flat from 23.2% last period.
sibc-msme-micro-small-share.direction: Share surged from 20.0% in Mar 2025 to 23.2% in Mar 2026, a 3.2 percentage point gain in one year.
sibc-msme-micro-small-share.inference: Micro & Small is the fastest-growing segment in industry credit, capturing share from large corporates at an unprecedented rate. The 3.2pp gain in one year is the largest share shift in this dataset.

sibc-msme-micro-small-yoy.title: Micro & Small at 33.1% YoY — highest in this window
sibc-msme-micro-small-yoy.observation: Micro & Small credit grew 33.1% year-on-year, the strongest reading in the six-period series.
sibc-msme-micro-small-yoy.direction: Growth accelerated from 30.4% last period to 33.1%, and from 8.9% in Mar 2025 to 33.1% in Mar 2026.
sibc-msme-micro-small-yoy.inference: Micro & Small credit is growing at nearly four times the rate of large corporates. This is the primary driver of the industry credit mix shift. The segment has sustained above-30% growth for three consecutive periods.

sibc-msme-size-yoy-spread.title: MSME-Large spread at 24.2pp — widest in this series
sibc-msme-size-yoy-spread.observation: MSME credit is growing 24.2 percentage points faster than large corporate credit.
sibc-msme-size-yoy-spread.direction: The spread widened from 22.6pp last period to 24.2pp, and from 2.1pp in Mar 2025 to 24.2pp in Mar 2026.
sibc-msme-size-yoy-spread.inference: The growth gap between MSME and large corporates has exploded from near-zero to 24.2pp in one year. This is a structural rebalancing of industry credit toward smaller borrowers, likely driven by policy mandates or risk appetite shifts.

sibc-textiles-sub-share-scan.title: Other Textiles at 44.6% share — largest sub-segment by far
sibc-textiles-sub-share-scan.observation: Other Textiles accounts for 44.6% of textiles credit, followed by Cotton Textiles at 36.5% and Man-Made Textiles at 17.2%.
sibc-textiles-sub-share-scan.direction: The spread between the largest and smallest sub-segments is 43.0 percentage points, with Jute Textiles at just 1.7%.
sibc-textiles-sub-share-scan.inference: Textiles credit is concentrated in Other Textiles and Cotton, which together hold 81.1% of the segment. Jute is negligible. The composition is stable — all four sub-segments are active with no status shifts.

sibc-textiles-sub-yoy-scan.title: Jute Textiles at 16.5% YoY — fastest growth in textiles
sibc-textiles-sub-yoy-scan.observation: Jute Textiles grew 16.5% year-on-year, the strongest performance among textiles sub-segments, followed by Other Textiles at 15.9%.
sibc-textiles-sub-yoy-scan.direction: All four sub-segments are strengthening, with growth ranging from 2.6% (Cotton) to 16.5% (Jute). Other Textiles and Cotton Textiles both shifted from active to strengthening.
sibc-textiles-sub-yoy-scan.inference: Textiles credit is accelerating across the board. Jute and Other Textiles are leading, but even Cotton — the slowest — is strengthening. The broad-based momentum suggests sector-wide demand recovery or policy support.

sector_mix.narrative: Services credit hit a record 19.0% YoY in March 2026, driven by explosive growth in Shipping (42.2%), Computer Software (39.0%), and NBFCs (26.3%). Within Trade, Wholesale is pulling away at 20.1% YoY and gaining share, while Retail lags at 11.5% — signalling banks are backing B2B supply chains over consumer retail. This is the broadest and strongest services credit expansion in this dataset, with nine of ten sub-sectors strengthening. For lenders, the opportunity is in capital-intensive and tech-enabled segments; the risk is in slower pockets like Transport Operators and Professional Services, where single-digit growth may mask emerging stress.

sibc-agriculture-share.title: Agriculture holds 12.4% of credit — stable near median
sibc-agriculture-share.observation: Agriculture's share of total bank credit stands at 12.43%, up 0.12 points from the prior period.
sibc-agriculture-share.direction: 12.30% → 12.43%, holding near the middle of its 5-period range (12.3%–12.6%).
sibc-agriculture-share.inference: Share stability despite accelerating growth signals agriculture is expanding in line with overall credit — no structural shift in bank allocation priorities.

sibc-agriculture-yoy.title: Agriculture credit surges 15.7% YoY — fastest on record
sibc-agriculture-yoy.observation: Agriculture credit grew 15.70% year-on-year in March 2026, the highest reading in this 6-period window.
sibc-agriculture-yoy.direction: 10.4% → 11.4% → 12.3% → 15.7% — accelerating sharply over three consecutive periods.
sibc-agriculture-yoy.inference: Agriculture lending has broken out of its 10–13% range. This is the strongest momentum on record, likely driven by policy push and rural credit demand — watch for sustained deployment or regulatory tailwinds.

sibc-industry-fy-acceleration.title: Industry credit accelerated 6.7pp on full-year basis — peak level
sibc-industry-fy-acceleration.observation: Industry credit's full-year acceleration stands at 6.7 percentage points, unchanged from the prior period and the maximum in this dataset.
sibc-industry-fy-acceleration.direction: Held steady at 6.7pp across all six periods — no further acceleration, but no deceleration either.
sibc-industry-fy-acceleration.inference: Industry credit growth has stabilised at its fastest full-year pace. The inflection is complete; banks are now sustaining elevated deployment rather than accelerating further.

sibc-industry-share.title: Industry share at 21.5% — stable near median
sibc-industry-share.observation: Industry's share of total credit is 21.52%, up 0.01 points from the prior period.
sibc-industry-share.direction: 22.0% → 21.7% → 21.5% → 21.5% — drifting down slightly but now stable.
sibc-industry-share.inference: Industry is growing fast (15% YoY) but not gaining share — other sectors are keeping pace. No structural reallocation toward industry despite strong growth.

sibc-industry-yoy.title: Industry credit hits 15.0% YoY — highest in dataset
sibc-industry-yoy.observation: Industry credit grew 14.96% year-on-year in March 2026, the strongest reading in this 6-period window.
sibc-industry-yoy.inference: Industry lending has doubled its growth rate in one year. This is the sharpest upturn on record — capex cycle revival or working capital expansion is underway. Watch for breadth across sub-sectors.

sibc-nbfc-sub-share-scan.title: PFIs gain share within NBFCs — now 16.9%, status strengthening
sibc-nbfc-sub-share-scan.observation: Within NBFC credit, Housing Finance Companies hold 18.1% and Public Financial Institutions 16.9% — a 1.2 point spread.
sibc-nbfc-sub-share-scan.direction: PFIs shifted from active to strengthening status, narrowing the gap with HFCs.
sibc-nbfc-sub-share-scan.inference: PFIs are gaining ground within the NBFC pool, likely driven by infrastructure or long-tenor project finance. HFCs remain dominant but PFIs are the faster mover.

sibc-nbfc-sub-yoy-scan.title: PFIs surge 52.5% YoY — 3× faster than HFCs at 15.5%
sibc-nbfc-sub-yoy-scan.observation: Public Financial Institutions grew 52.5% year-on-year, while Housing Finance Companies grew 15.5% — a 37 point spread.
sibc-nbfc-sub-yoy-scan.direction: Both sub-sectors are strengthening, but PFIs are accelerating far faster than HFCs.
sibc-nbfc-sub-yoy-scan.inference: PFI credit is exploding — likely infrastructure or government-linked project finance. HFCs are growing steadily but PFIs are the outlier story within NBFCs. Watch for PFI share to keep rising.

sibc-personal-loans-share.title: Personal loans at 32.6% of credit — down 0.3pp
sibc-personal-loans-share.observation: Personal loans account for 32.60% of total bank credit, down 0.28 points from the prior period.
sibc-personal-loans-share.inference: Personal loans are growing fast (16.2% YoY) but losing share — agriculture, industry, and services are outpacing retail. No structural shift yet, but watch for continued erosion if other sectors sustain momentum.

sibc-personal-loans-yoy.title: Personal loans grow 16.2% YoY — fastest in dataset
sibc-personal-loans-yoy.observation: Personal loans credit grew 16.24% year-on-year in March 2026, the highest reading in this 6-period window.
sibc-personal-loans-yoy.inference: Retail credit has re-accelerated after a mid-2025 slowdown. This is the strongest growth on record in this window — unsecured, cards, and vehicle loans likely driving the upturn. Watch for regulatory scrutiny if pace holds.

sibc-services-fy-acceleration.title: Services credit accelerated 7.0pp on full-year basis — peak level
sibc-services-fy-acceleration.observation: Services credit's full-year acceleration stands at 7.0 percentage points, unchanged from the prior period and the maximum in this dataset.
sibc-services-fy-acceleration.direction: Held steady at 7.0pp across all six periods — no further acceleration, but no deceleration either.
sibc-services-fy-acceleration.inference: Services credit growth has stabilised at its fastest full-year pace. The inflection is complete; banks are now sustaining elevated deployment rather than accelerating further.

sibc-services-share.title: Services share hits 28.5% — highest on record
sibc-services-share.observation: Services account for 28.47% of total bank credit, up 0.37 points from the prior period and the highest in this 5-period window.
sibc-services-share.inference: Services are gaining share while growing fast — a compounding advantage. NBFCs, trade, and other services are driving the shift. This is the strongest structural reallocation in the dataset.

sibc-services-share-scan.title: NBFCs dominate services at 34.1% — status strengthening
sibc-services-share-scan.observation: Within services credit, NBFCs hold 34.1%, Trade 22.7%, and Other Services 21.0% — a 33.9 point spread between top and bottom.
sibc-services-share-scan.direction: NBFCs shifted from active to strengthening status, pulling further ahead of Trade and Other Services.
sibc-services-share-scan.inference: NBFCs are the dominant force within services and accelerating. Trade and Other Services are stable but NBFCs are the growth engine — driven by PFI surge (52.5% YoY) and HFC expansion. Watch for NBFC share to keep rising.

sibc-services-yoy.title: Services credit at 19.0% YoY — highest on record
sibc-services-yoy.observation: Services credit grew 19.0% YoY in March 2026, the strongest reading in this 6-period window.
sibc-services-yoy.direction: Growth accelerated from 16.3% last period to 19.0% — a 2.73pp jump that pushed the rate to its historical maximum.
sibc-services-yoy.inference: Services is now the fastest-growing major sector, outpacing industry and agriculture. This momentum is broad-based across sub-sectors and signals sustained corporate and commercial credit demand — a structural tailwind for NBFCs and banks with strong services exposure.

sibc-services-yoy-scan.title: Shipping surged to 42.2% YoY — Computer Software at 39.0%
sibc-services-yoy-scan.observation: Nine of ten services sub-sectors are strengthening, with Shipping (42.2%), Computer Software (39.0%), and NBFCs (26.3%) leading growth.
sibc-services-yoy-scan.direction: Shipping flipped from active to strengthening; Computer Software moved from strengthening to active. The spread between top and bottom performers widened to 32.5pp.
sibc-services-yoy-scan.inference: Growth is concentrating in capital-intensive and tech-driven segments. Shipping's acceleration reflects trade finance and fleet expansion demand; Computer Software's sustained strength confirms enterprise IT spending resilience. Transport Operators (9.6%) and Professional Services (11.4%) lag — watch for credit quality stress in these slower pockets.

sibc-trade-sub-share-scan.title: Wholesale Trade holds 56.4% share — Retail at 43.6%
sibc-trade-sub-share-scan.observation: Wholesale Trade accounts for 56.4% of total Trade credit, while Retail Trade holds 43.6%.
sibc-trade-sub-share-scan.direction: Wholesale strengthening, Retail weakening — the 12.8pp spread reflects diverging momentum within the Trade segment.
sibc-trade-sub-share-scan.inference: Wholesale's share dominance and strengthening status indicate banks are prioritising larger distributors and B2B supply chains over consumer-facing retail. Retail's weakening share suggests either slower credit uptake or tighter underwriting in the consumer retail segment.

sibc-trade-sub-yoy-scan.title: Wholesale Trade at 20.1% YoY — Retail at 11.5%
sibc-trade-sub-yoy-scan.observation: Wholesale Trade grew 20.1% YoY, while Retail Trade grew 11.5% — both strengthening but with an 8.6pp growth gap.
sibc-trade-sub-yoy-scan.direction: Both sub-sectors moved from active to strengthening, but Wholesale accelerated faster, widening the spread from prior periods.
sibc-trade-sub-yoy-scan.inference: Wholesale's 20% growth rate aligns with the broader services surge and reflects strong working capital demand from distributors and traders. Retail's slower 11.5% growth suggests consumer-facing retail credit is lagging — a potential early signal of cautious consumer spending or tighter retail lending standards.

retail.narrative: Retail credit is in a structural shift: gold loans continue to grow at 123% YoY for the sixth straight period, while vehicle loans have re-accelerated to 18.6% — more than double the 8.6% rate a year ago. Housing remains the largest category at 48.3% share but is losing ground to faster-growing segments, despite hitting its highest YoY rate (11.5%) in this window. Unsecured categories are mixed: Other Personal Loans have re-accelerated to 13% after a weak FY25, but credit cards (3.5%) and consumer durables (-5.3%) are lagging. The 128.5pp growth spread between gold and consumer durables is the widest on record — retail credit is concentrating in secured, high-velocity products (gold, vehicles) while discretionary unsecured lending remains subdued.

sibc-pl-cc-below10-streak.title: Credit cards sub-10% YoY for 3 straight periods — slowdown entrenched
sibc-pl-cc-below10-streak.observation: Credit card growth has remained below 10% YoY for 3 consecutive periods, the longest such streak in this dataset.
sibc-pl-cc-below10-streak.direction: Streak extended from 2 periods in Feb 2026 to 3 periods in Mar 2026, with growth at 13.0% → 11.2% → 10.6% → 1.5% → 1.7% → 3.5%.
sibc-pl-cc-below10-streak.inference: The credit card segment has shifted from double-digit expansion to sustained single-digit growth. This entrenched slowdown signals either tighter underwriting, regulatory headwinds, or demand saturation — lenders should reassess portfolio mix and acquisition strategies.

sibc-pl-cc-share.title: Credit card share at 4.24% — lowest on record, down from 5.0%
sibc-pl-cc-share.observation: Credit cards now represent 4.24% of personal loans, the lowest share in this 6-period window.
sibc-pl-cc-share.direction: Share declined steadily from 5.0% in Jan 2025 to 4.24% in Mar 2026, down 0.06pp from prior period.
sibc-pl-cc-share.inference: Credit cards are losing ground within personal loans despite overall retail credit expansion. Other segments — particularly gold and housing — are capturing a larger share of incremental lending, reflecting a structural shift in lender and borrower preferences.

sibc-pl-cc-yoy.title: Credit card growth at 3.55% YoY — up from 1.75% but still weak
sibc-pl-cc-yoy.observation: Credit card outstanding grew 3.55% YoY in Mar 2026, up 1.80pp from the prior period's 1.75%.
sibc-pl-cc-yoy.direction: Growth trajectory: 13.0% → 11.2% → 10.6% → 1.5% → 1.7% → 3.5%, showing a modest uptick after hitting a low of 1.5% in Jan 2026.
sibc-pl-cc-yoy.inference: While growth has ticked up from its trough, the 3.55% rate remains far below the 10%+ levels seen a year ago. This is a stabilisation, not a recovery — credit card portfolios remain under pressure and are unlikely to drive retail credit growth in the near term.

sibc-pl-consumer-durables-neg-streak.title: Consumer durables negative for 4 straight periods — longest contraction streak
sibc-pl-consumer-durables-neg-streak.observation: Consumer durables have contracted YoY for 4 consecutive periods, the longest negative streak in this dataset.
sibc-pl-consumer-durables-neg-streak.direction: Streak extended from 3 periods in Feb 2026 to 4 periods in Mar 2026, with growth at -2.4% → 2.6% → -1.0% → -4.0% → -9.8% → -5.3%.
sibc-pl-consumer-durables-neg-streak.inference: Consumer durables lending is in sustained decline, signaling weak demand for big-ticket discretionary purchases. This segment is structurally challenged — lenders should expect continued headwinds and consider reallocating capital to higher-growth retail categories.

sibc-pl-consumer-durables-share.title: Consumer durables at 0.32% share — lowest on record, down from 0.4%
sibc-pl-consumer-durables-share.observation: Consumer durables now account for 0.32% of personal loans, the smallest share in this 6-period window.
sibc-pl-consumer-durables-share.direction: Share fell from 0.4% in Jan 2025 to 0.32% in Mar 2026, down 0.01pp from prior period.
sibc-pl-consumer-durables-share.inference: Consumer durables have become a negligible part of the personal loan mix. The combination of shrinking share and sustained contraction confirms this is a structurally declining segment — not a cyclical dip.

sibc-pl-consumer-durables-yoy.title: Consumer durables -5.34% YoY — contraction easing from -9.78%
sibc-pl-consumer-durables-yoy.observation: Consumer durables outstanding contracted 5.34% YoY in Mar 2026, an improvement of 4.44pp from the prior period's -9.78%.
sibc-pl-consumer-durables-yoy.direction: Growth trajectory: -2.4% → 2.6% → -1.0% → -4.0% → -9.8% → -5.3%, showing the decline is moderating after hitting -9.8% in Feb 2026.
sibc-pl-consumer-durables-yoy.inference: The pace of contraction is slowing, but the segment remains in decline. This is a less-bad reading, not a turnaround — consumer durables lending is still shrinking and shows no signs of returning to growth.

sibc-pl-education-share.title: Education loans at 2.25% share — lowest on record, down from 2.3%
sibc-pl-education-share.observation: Education loans represent 2.25% of personal loans, the smallest share in this 6-period window.
sibc-pl-education-share.direction: Share edged down from 2.3% across Jan-Feb 2026 to 2.25% in Mar 2026, down 0.04pp from prior period.
sibc-pl-education-share.inference: Education loans are holding steady in absolute terms but losing share as other retail segments grow faster. This is a stable but slow-growth niche — lenders focused on education lending face limited upside in the current environment.

sibc-pl-education-yoy.title: Education loans at 13.38% YoY — steady but slowest in this window
sibc-pl-education-yoy.observation: Education loans grew 13.38% YoY in Mar 2026, the lowest growth rate in this 6-period dataset.
sibc-pl-education-yoy.direction: Growth decelerated steadily from 15.9% in Jan 2025 to 13.38% in Mar 2026, down 0.34pp from prior period.
sibc-pl-education-yoy.inference: Education lending is growing at a healthy double-digit pace but momentum is fading. The gradual slowdown suggests demand is moderating or lenders are tightening underwriting — this segment is stable but no longer accelerating.

sibc-pl-fd-advances-share.title: FD-backed advances at 2.44% share — highest on record, up from 2.3%
sibc-pl-fd-advances-share.observation: Advances against fixed deposits now account for 2.44% of personal loans, the highest share in this 6-period window.
sibc-pl-fd-advances-share.direction: Share rose from 2.3% in Jan 2025 to 2.44% in Mar 2026, up 0.17pp from prior period.
sibc-pl-fd-advances-share.inference: FD-backed lending is gaining share, reflecting either increased borrower preference for low-cost secured credit or lender appetite for low-risk collateralised exposure. This is a defensive shift — growth is concentrating in the safest retail segment.

sibc-pl-fd-advances-yoy.title: FD-backed advances at 19.22% YoY — highest on record, up from 14.07%
sibc-pl-fd-advances-yoy.observation: Advances against fixed deposits grew 19.22% YoY in Mar 2026, the fastest growth rate in this 6-period dataset.
sibc-pl-fd-advances-yoy.direction: Growth accelerated sharply from 14.07% in Feb 2026 to 19.22% in Mar 2026, up 5.16pp — the strongest reading in this window.
sibc-pl-fd-advances-yoy.inference: FD-backed lending is surging, driven by borrowers seeking low-cost liquidity and lenders prioritising fully collateralised exposure. This is the fastest-growing retail segment and signals a flight to safety in personal lending.

sibc-pl-gold-pos-streak.title: Gold loans positive for 6 straight periods — longest growth streak on record
sibc-pl-gold-pos-streak.observation: Gold loans have grown YoY for 6 consecutive periods, the longest uninterrupted positive streak in this dataset.
sibc-pl-gold-pos-streak.direction: Streak extended from 5 periods in Feb 2026 to 6 periods in Mar 2026, covering the entire observation window.
sibc-pl-gold-pos-streak.inference: Gold loans are in a sustained expansion phase with no sign of slowdown. This is the most consistent growth story in retail credit — lenders should view gold as a core portfolio driver and allocate capital accordingly.

sibc-pl-gold-share.title: Gold loans at 6.63% share — highest on record, doubled from 3.0%
sibc-pl-gold-share.observation: Gold loans now represent 6.63% of personal loans, the highest share in this 6-period window.
sibc-pl-gold-share.direction: Share surged from 3.0% in Jan 2025 to 6.63% in Mar 2026, up 0.33pp from prior period — more than doubling in 15 months.
sibc-pl-gold-share.inference: Gold loans are capturing an outsized share of incremental personal lending, reflecting strong borrower demand and lender appetite for asset-backed exposure. This structural shift is reshaping the retail credit mix — gold is now a top-tier segment by share and momentum.

sibc-pl-gold-yoy.title: Gold loans at 123% YoY — still triple-digit despite 5pp deceleration
sibc-pl-gold-yoy.observation: Gold loans grew 123.1% YoY in Mar 2026, down from 127.9% in Feb 2026.
sibc-pl-gold-yoy.direction: Growth decelerated 4.8 points period-on-period (127.9% → 123.1%), but remains near the median of the six-period range.
sibc-pl-gold-yoy.inference: Triple-digit growth persists for the sixth consecutive period. The slight deceleration does not signal a trend break — gold lending remains the fastest-growing retail segment by a wide margin, driven by sustained demand and aggressive lender expansion.

sibc-pl-housing-share.title: Housing share falls to 48.3% — lowest in this window
sibc-pl-housing-share.observation: Housing loans now account for 48.3% of personal loans, the lowest share in six periods.
sibc-pl-housing-share.direction: Share declined from 50.4% in Mar 2025 to 48.3% in Mar 2026, losing 2.1 points year-on-year.
sibc-pl-housing-share.inference: Housing is losing ground structurally despite growing at 11.5% YoY. Faster-growing segments — gold at 123%, vehicles at 19% — are capturing a larger slice of incremental retail credit. Housing remains the largest category but is no longer the growth driver.

sibc-pl-housing-yoy.title: Housing loans hit 11.5% YoY — highest in six periods
sibc-pl-housing-yoy.observation: Housing loans grew 11.5% YoY in Mar 2026, the fastest rate in this window.
sibc-pl-housing-yoy.direction: Growth accelerated from 10.7% in Mar 2025 to 11.5% in Mar 2026, and ticked up 0.5 points from 11.0% in Feb 2026.
sibc-pl-housing-yoy.inference: Housing growth is strengthening on an absolute basis, but the 11.5% rate is being outpaced by other retail segments. The share loss confirms housing is growing slower than the overall personal loan pool — a structural shift toward higher-velocity products.

sibc-pl-other-share.title: Other Personal Loans at 25.0% share — lowest on record
sibc-pl-other-share.observation: Other Personal Loans account for 25.0% of personal loans, the lowest share in six periods.
sibc-pl-other-share.direction: Share fell from 25.7% in Mar 2025 to 25.0% in Mar 2026, losing 0.7 points year-on-year.
sibc-pl-other-share.inference: Despite accelerating YoY growth (now 13.0%), Other PL is losing share to gold and vehicle loans. This category — which includes unsecured personal loans — is growing but not fast enough to hold its composition weight in a rapidly expanding retail pool.

sibc-pl-other-yoy.title: Other Personal Loans surge to 13.0% YoY — fastest in this window
sibc-pl-other-yoy.observation: Other Personal Loans grew 13.0% YoY in Mar 2026, the highest rate in six periods.
sibc-pl-other-yoy.direction: Growth accelerated sharply from 7.5% in Mar 2025 to 13.0% in Mar 2026, and rose 0.9 points from 12.1% in Feb 2026.
sibc-pl-other-yoy.inference: Other PL has re-accelerated after a weak FY25 base (7–8% range). The 13% rate signals renewed appetite for unsecured lending, but the category is still being outgrown by gold and vehicles — hence the share loss. Watch for sustained acceleration to confirm a structural recovery.

sibc-pl-share-advances-share.title: Advances against shares at 0.2% — negligible and stable
sibc-pl-share-advances-share.observation: Advances against shares and bonds account for 0.2% of personal loans, unchanged from prior period.
sibc-pl-share-advances-share.direction: Share has held steady at 0.2% across the six-period window, with no meaningful movement.
sibc-pl-share-advances-share.inference: This is a rounding-error category with no strategic relevance. The 4.6% YoY growth is noise — the absolute size is too small to influence retail credit dynamics.

sibc-pl-share-advances-yoy.title: Advances against shares at 4.6% YoY — collapsed from 33% a year ago
sibc-pl-share-advances-yoy.observation: Advances against shares grew 4.6% YoY in Mar 2026, up from 2.8% in Feb 2026.
sibc-pl-share-advances-yoy.direction: Growth has collapsed from 18.7% in Mar 2025 to 4.6% in Mar 2026, though it ticked up 1.8 points period-on-period.
sibc-pl-share-advances-yoy.inference: This category saw a sharp deceleration over the past year, likely reflecting tighter underwriting or reduced demand for margin lending. The recent uptick to 4.6% is too small to signal a reversal — the category remains in low single-digit growth and structurally irrelevant.

sibc-pl-share-scan.title: Housing 48.3%, Other PL 25.0%, Vehicles 10.6% — top three hold 84% of retail
sibc-pl-share-scan.observation: Housing (48.3%), Other Personal Loans (25.0%), and Vehicle Loans (10.6%) account for 84% of personal loans.
sibc-pl-share-scan.direction: The top three categories dominate, with a 48.2 percentage point spread between the largest (Housing) and smallest (Advances against shares at 0.2%).
sibc-pl-share-scan.inference: Retail credit is concentrated in three products: secured housing, unsecured personal loans, and auto finance. Gold (6.6% share, not in top three) is the fastest-growing segment but still small in absolute terms. Credit cards (3.5% YoY) and education (negative growth) are lagging — focus remains on housing, vehicles, and gold for incremental deployment.

sibc-pl-vehicle-share.title: Vehicle loans at 10.6% share — stable despite 19% YoY growth
sibc-pl-vehicle-share.observation: Vehicle loans account for 10.6% of personal loans, down marginally from 10.7% in Feb 2026.
sibc-pl-vehicle-share.direction: Share has held steady in the 10.4–10.7% range across six periods, with no meaningful trend.
sibc-pl-vehicle-share.inference: Vehicles are growing at 18.6% YoY — the third-fastest rate after gold and fixed deposit advances — but the share is flat because the overall retail pool is expanding at a similar pace. This is a stable, high-growth category with no structural share gain or loss.

sibc-pl-vehicle-yoy.title: Vehicle loans hit 18.6% YoY — more than doubled from 8.6% a year ago
sibc-pl-vehicle-yoy.observation: Vehicle loans grew 18.6% YoY in Mar 2026, the fastest rate in six periods.
sibc-pl-vehicle-yoy.direction: Growth surged from 8.6% in Mar 2025 to 18.6% in Mar 2026, and accelerated 1.5 points from 17.1% in Feb 2026.
sibc-pl-vehicle-yoy.inference: Auto finance has re-accelerated sharply after a weak FY25 base. The 18.6% rate is the highest in this window and signals strong demand for vehicle loans — likely driven by new model launches, improved consumer sentiment, and competitive pricing. This is a confirmed inflection, not a one-period spike.

sibc-pl-yoy-scan.title: Gold 123%, Vehicles 19%, Housing 11% — growth spread widening to 128pp
sibc-pl-yoy-scan.observation: Gold loans lead at 123.1% YoY, followed by advances against fixed deposits (19.2%) and vehicle loans (18.6%). Consumer durables contracted 5.3%.
sibc-pl-yoy-scan.direction: The spread between the fastest (gold) and slowest (consumer durables) segment is 128.5 percentage points, the widest in this window.
sibc-pl-yoy-scan.inference: Retail credit growth is bifurcating: gold and vehicles are surging, housing is steady at 11%, and credit cards (3.5%) and consumer durables (-5.3%) are lagging. Six of nine categories are strengthening, but the gains are concentrated in secured products (gold, vehicles, housing). Unsecured categories — cards, durables — are underperforming, signaling lender caution or demand weakness in discretionary spending.
